category_title: Chamorro 5th-grade student with ADHD and literacy challenges
minimal_context: Juan is sitting in the 5th-grade classroom using paper and pencil to write a summary of a recently read text.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please use your paper and pencil to write a summary of the text we just read.

STUDENT: (Scribbles very quickly for a few moments and pushes the paper forward) I'm done.

TUTOR: You only wrote a few words. Can you add more details about what happened?

STUDENT: No, I'm a poor writer.

TUTOR: Why do you say that? Just keep trying to write.

STUDENT: (Erasing aggressively) Because my penmanship is bad! I keep spelling things wrong and the letters are messy. If it's not neat, it's not good writing, so I don't want to do it.

options.A: Tell the student that his decision to stop writing the summary is a good idea, validating his concerns about messy letters and spelling mistakes.
options.B: Reread the text slowly, using both English and Chamorro, reading each line first in English and then adding Chamorro explanations to the author's writing.
options.C: Move the student beyond anxiety about penmanship to caring about the content of his summary, deferring refinement of mechanics to the writing process.
options.D: Respond with enthusiastic praise, telling the student, "Another one already? No way! I’m so proud of you! Good job, keep going with the summary."
